journey.unlocks: The ability to name what you already felt but couldn’t articulate — and act on it without guilt.
journey.identity_shift: From person who overrides gut signals to preserve politeness — to person who treats fear as reliable information.

pillars.name: Mindset
pillars.why: This book is the scientific and philosophical foundation for the ETKM mindset layer — denial is the primary threat, and the Protector identity begins with accepting that risk is real and signals are present.

pillars.name: Awareness
pillars.why: Pre-contact awareness is a trainable biological system, not a personality trait. PINS are observable, consistent, and precede violence — ETKM trains their recognition through deliberate practice and scenario work.

pillars.name: Tactics
pillars.why: Upstream tactics — signal labeling, manipulation recognition, verbal boundary-setting — prevent encounters that physical technique can only respond to. Train the upstream first.

principles.text: Your gut feeling is rapid pattern recognition. The cue is real. Name it before dismissing it.
Violence is predictable. Study the patterns. The signals are almost always present before the event.
Denial disables the system designed to protect you. Replace denial with preparation.
Six manipulation tactics predators use consistently. Learn them by name — they lose power when named.
No is a complete sentence. No explanation. No negotiation. Practice saying it.
Persistence is a risk marker. It tells you exactly how someone views your right to say no.
Safety is a system — contacts, exits, routines, plans. Build it before you need it.
Politeness can be weaponized against you. Your comfort with social friction is a self-defense skill.
Act on patterns early. By the time proof exists, the cheapest windows have already closed.
Guilt is not a safety metric. Choose your outcome over your feeling.

this_week: Map two exits in every space you enter today.
Name one 'something’s off' cue you notice — just name it, no journaling required.
Practice a clean 'No' at low stakes once today.
this_month: Learn all six manipulation tactics by name.
Teach one family member about the tactics and what to look for.
Practice verbal boundary-setting under pressure — in class and in daily life.
Identify one real-life interview attempt you recognized in the wild.
permanent: Treat fear as information. Investigate before dismissing.
Never override discomfort to preserve social comfort.
Build a real safety system — named contacts, exit routines, plans.
Evaluate behavior over appearance in every unfamiliar encounter.
